content.full_text:
California 
dum 
Advice File -- Section 86203 
fAIR POUTICAl PRACTICES COMMISSION 
Jeanne Pritchard 
Date 
6/2/83 
Terry McGann, a lobbyist, asked the following 
question: 
Last week his client, Family Health Program, sent 
invitations to a number of legislative officials for a 
reception. 
The pro rata benefit will be more than $10.00. 
Without Mr. McGann's knowledge, Family Health Program listed 
two telephone numbers on the invitations for RSVP's -- their 
own and his. 
As soon as he learned of it, he notified 
the client that he would be unable to talk with people 
responding because that would constitute "arranging" a 
prohibited gift. 
He asked if he could avoid participating 
in "arranging" by informing any callers that he is not 
involved in any way in organizing the event and that they 
must call the client directly to discuss any aspect of 
the event. 
As that approach is consistent with the analysis to 
question (8) in the IGA Opinion (No. 81-003, 7 FPPC 1), I 
told him that he could avoid violating Section 86203 by 
handling any calls he got in that way. 


California 
r ndum 
Advice File: 
Sections 842ll(j) and 
84303 
fAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION 
J~Pritchard 
Dote 
7/1/83 
Reporting a "series of payments" for a single service or 
product; 
reporting payments made by a paid solicitor for 
signature gathering. 
On June 29, 1983, I met with Dimitri Viripaeff, 
treasurer for Assemblyman Sebastiani's reapportionment 
qualification committee, "Californians for Fair and 
Independent Representation/Sebastiani Committee." 
We discussed, generally, record keeping require-
ments, period covered and due date, and disclosure require-
ments. 
Specifically, I gave him the following advice 
regarding reporting expenditures: 
Pursuant to Section 842ll(j), multiple payments 
totaling $100 or more during the reporting period may be 
reported as a single entry on the campaign statement, and 
a notation should be made in the committee's records to 
account for the total amount reported as a lump sum on 
the campaign statement. 
Pursuant to Section 84303, when a paid solicitor 
(a firm) makes payments to individuals with whom it 
contracts to collect signatures for the committee's 
qualification effort, the committee must itemize each 
individual to whom the firm pays $100 or more during the 
qualification period. 


State of California 
Memorandum 
To 
Advice File: 
Sections 842ll(j) and 
84303 
Date 
7/1/83 
From 
: 
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION 
J.Pritchard 
Subject: 
Reporting a "series of payments" for a single service or 
product; 
reporting payments made by a paid solicitor for 
signature gathering. 
On June 29, 1983, I met with Dimitri Viripaeff, 
treasurer for Assemblyman Sebastiani's reapportionment 
qualification committee, "Californians for Fair and 
Independent Representation/Sebastiani Committee." 
We discussed, generally, record keeping require-
ments, period covered and due date, and disclosure require-
ments. 
Specifically, I gave him the following advice 
regarding reporting expenditures: 
Pursuant to Section 842ll(j), multiple payments 
totaling $100 or more during the reporting period may be 
reported as a single entry on the campaign statement, and 
a notation should be made in the committee's records to 
account for the total amount reported as a lump sum on 
the campaign statement. 
Pursuant to Section 84303, when a paid solicitor 
(a firm) makes payments to individuals with whom it 
contracts to collect signatures for the committee's 
qualification effort, the committee must itemize each 
individual to whom the firm pays $100 or more during the 
qualification period. 


P.O. BOX 807 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 ••• 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814 
Administration 
(916) 322·5662 
322·5660 
Rosie Vandergriff 
Accounting Department 
Murdoch, Mockler & Associates 
1130 K Street, Suite 210 
Sacramento, CA 
95814 
Dear Rosie: 
Executive/legal 
322,5901 
Enforcement 
322·6441 
July 8, 1983 
Statements of Economic Interest 
322·6444 
Re: 
Advice No. A-83-170 
This is in reply to your letter dated June 28, 1983, and to 
confirm our previous conversation regarding the proper method of 
reporting payments received for lobbying services by the firm of 
Murdoch, Mockler & Associates from the Los Angeles Unified School 
District (LAUSD). 
You indicated that the LAUSD cannot contract with a firm but 
can only contract with individuals and that, therefore, the 
payments to Murdoch, Mockler & Associates are made to the firm's 
lobbyist. 
Previously, the payments were made to John Mockler; 
currently they are being made to James Murdoch. 
Mr. Murdoch is 
then remitting to Murdoch, Mockler the payment received from LAUSD. 
Because Mr. Murdoch is receiving direct payments from LAUSD, 
he must report receipt of the payments on his Lobbyist Report (Form 
620) in Part II, Column A -- "Payments Received in Connection with 
Lobbying Activity/Salaries, Fees and Retainers," and Mr. Murdoch 
must list LAUSD as the employer. 
In order to clarify that he is 
not personally keeping the payments received from LAUSD, he may 
enter a notation explaining that he is receiving the payments 
directly from LAUSD and is then turning the payments over to the 
firm. 
The firm must report receipt of the payments from Mr. 
Murdoch on its Form 680 -
"Payments Received by Business Entities 
Retained to Lobby," showing Mr. Murdoch as the person making the 
payment. 
On the firm's Form 680, you may include an explanation 
that the payment received from Mr. Murdoch is actually a payment to 
the lobbyist from LAUSD that is being given to the firm. 
Regarding the letter from the Secretary of State's office, you and 
I previously discussed the proper method of correcting the problem 
which exists because of Mr. Mockler's receipt of the payments from 
LAUSD. 
You indicated that Mr. Mockler would amend his Registration 


Rosie Vandergriff 
July 8, 1983 
Page 2 
Statement to show LAUSD as a direct employer, and would amend his 
Lobbyist Report (Form 620) to show receipt of the payments directly 
from LAUSD. 
In addition, Murdoch, Mockler & Associates' Form 680 
will be amended to show receipt of the payment in question from Mr. 
Mockler, rather than from LAUSD. 
Again, all of these entires on 
the LObbyist and Lobbyist Employer Reports may be fully explained 
in footnotes. 
After discussing this unusual situation with other staff, we 
have determined that this method of disclosure provides the most 
accurate and clear information about the transactions. 
I realize 
that this method varies somewhat from that which we previously 
discussed, so please give me a call if it is not clear, or if you 
have any additional questions. 
cc: Political Reform Division 
Secretary of State's Office 
JP/bb 
Sincerely, 
Jeanne Pritchard 
Political Reform Consultant 


MURDOCH, MOCKLER & ASSOCIATES 
.JAMES L. ML..;RC'OCH 
DAVID :... 
WAl...~A-;-H 
June 28, 1983 
Jean Pritchard 
Fair Political Practices Commission 
Technical Assistance & Analysis Division 
P. O. Box 807 
Sacramento, CA 95804 
Dear Jean: 
1;30 X STRE::::;-, Sl.J~TE 210 
SACRAMENTO. CALIFORNIA 95814 
(916) 44'-3300 
Pursuant to our phone conversation last this week, this letter wil I hopefully 
clear up the confusion created by the reporting of monies received by Murdoch, 
Mockler & Associates from the Los Angeles Unified School District. 
Los Angeles Unified School District (LAUSD) has a contract with James L. 
Murdoch (until January 31, 1983, that contract was with John Mockler, who is no 
longer with Murdoch, Mock I er & Associ ates). 
My understand j ng is that LAUSD 
cannot draw up a contract with a firm; all their contracts have to be wIth 
individuals. 
Therefore, even though LAUSD makes out its checks to the indiv!aua/ (James L. 
Murdoch), al I monies received are deposited Into the Murdoch, Mockler & 
Associates account. The lobbyist James L. Murdoch is merely acting as an 
intermediary for LAUSD In transferring the payment from LAUSD to Murdoch, 
Mockl er & Assoc i ates. 
If you need any further information, please feel free to call -- it does get 
quite confusing! 
Sincerely,

0' ~. :Gf': 
Rosie Vandergriff, Supe~ 
Accounting Department 


March Fong Eu 
Secretary of State 
1230 J Street 
P. O. Box 1467 
n:::r,:):'..1' -;?acr(l.ffiffit~lifomia 95807 
Political Reform Division 
(916) 322-4880

- .. ,I .... :""1\ JJc,t.~-
lVISION

/

1. •• r\1 
April 26, 1983 
Mr. David W. Koch, Deputy Controller 
Los Angeles Unified School District 
t:.50 NOtt;l Grap.d ,4,. venue 
.. Los Angeles, CA 90012 
Dear Mr. Koch: 
Thank you for your Form 660L, executed on October 19, 1982, for the reporting 
period july 1, 1982 thrcugh September 30,1982. As required by the Political 
Reform Act of 1974, we have reviewed your report to determine whether it 
conforms on its face with the disdosure requirements. 
Part II-Individual Lob~::.dst Employed 
On Part II, the name of John Mockler is listeG. Our understanding is that 
you retain the firm of Murdock, Mockler &: Associates, Inc. Therefore, 
in the future, please list the firm together with any attributable payments 
on Part III-Payments, Section B: Other Payments to Influence LegislatiVE: 
or Administrative Action. 
If you have any questions, please call Debra McCastle at (916) 322-0392. 
Sincerely, 
~~:v/ 
MARIAN ASH, Assistant Chief 
Political Reform Division

sections.question_synthetic: This document contains multiple unrelated FPPC advice memoranda from 1983 covering: (1) whether a lobbyist can avoid violating gift arrangement rules by declining to participate in a client-organized reception; (2) how to report multiple payments and paid solicitor expenditures on campaign statements; and (3) the proper reporting method for lobbying payments received by a firm's individual lobbyist acting as an intermediary.
sections.conclusion_synthetic: FPPC provided three separate pieces of advice: (1) a lobbyist can avoid violating Section 86203 by informing callers they are not involved in organizing an event and directing them to contact the client directly; (2) multiple payments totaling $100+ may be reported as a single entry with notation in records, and paid solicitors' payments to individuals must itemize those paid $100+ individually; (3) lobbying payments received by an individual lobbyist must be reported on Form 620 with notation that they are being remitted to the firm, and the firm reports receipt on Form 680 from the individual.
sections.parsing_notes: Format: modern; LLM: Document contains multiple independent advice letters and correspondence rather than a single cohesive response. Includes incoming request from Murdoch, Mockler & Associates and follow-up FPPC response letter (A-83-170), plus separate memoranda on campaign reporting and earlier lobbyist gift arrangement advice. Quality and clarity vary across sections.

CONCLUSION: FPPC provided three separate pieces of advice: (1) a lobbyist can avoid violating Section 86203 by informing callers they are not involved in organizing an event and directing them to contact the client directly; (2) multiple payments totaling $100+ may be reported as a single entry with notation in records, and paid solicitors' payments to individuals must itemize those paid $100+ individually; (3) lobbying payments received by an individual lobbyist must be reported on Form 620 with notation that they are being remitted to the firm, and the firm reports receipt on Form 680 from the individual.

The pro rata benefit will be more than $10.00. Without Mr. McGann's knowledge, Family Health Program listed two telephone numbers on the invitations for RSVP's -- their own and his. As soon as he learned of it, he notified the client that he would be unable to talk with people responding because that would constitute "arranging" a prohibited gift. We discussed, generally, record keeping require- ments, period covered and due date, and disclosure require- ments. embedding.summary: This document is a compilation of four separate FPPC advice letters and related correspondence from mid-1983 addressing lobbying gift arrangements, campaign finance reporting of multiple payments, and proper disclosure of lobbying payments made through intermediary individual lobbyists.
